title: After months of overfitting and failed live runs, the model finally held up today.
author: morriase
selftext:
I’ve probably spent more time in Python notebooks in the last year than actually watching price.

If you’ve tried pushing ML into live FX trading, you may know how brutal the transition is. Usually, the backtests look solid, then you go live and spreads, slippage, and noise expose every weakness instantly.

I stopped trying to engineer the “perfect” entry and moved toward ensemble logic instead — letting different models handle different parts of the problem. Right now I’m combining an LSTM for sequence behavior with XGBoost for structured features.

Today wasn’t spectacular in terms of profit. It was just… stable.

https://reddit.com/link/1r2xaw7/video/0cmju4uz63jg1/player

I’m still running conservative sizing while I tune risk and drawdown control, but it finally feels like I’m testing a system instead of fighting randomness.

Curious how others are navigating current conditions. Anyone else blending ML with traditional structure, or sticking to discretionary setups?

comments.author: Minute_Disk_2860
comments.body: ML only works when the underlying distribution of all past, present and future trades is stationary. With all the random effects introduced by the political, economic, geopolitical uncertainty, and blatant manipulations by WS, you are trying to predict the unpredictable. It is a rigged game.

comments.author: Minute_Disk_2860
comments.body: I played this game 6-7 years ago. Did worse than manual trading.

comments.author: Automatic-Essay2175
comments.body:
If your live trades don’t match your backtested trades, it’s not because backtesting is inaccurate, it’s because *your* backtest is inaccurate. 

Everything clicked for me when I stopped using ML. More models is not the answer. 

Find a working strategy and then automate it with rules.

comments.author: sedidrl
comments.body: How's your experience with FX, did you try crypto as well? Thinking of adding it to [https://github.com/TorchTrade/torchtrade](https://github.com/TorchTrade/torchtrade)

comments.author: Backtester4Ever
comments.body: Stability is the real milestone, not profit. If spreads and slippage no longer break the model instantly, that’s progress. The usual trap with ML isn’t model choice, it’s execution realism. Ensembles can smooth behavior, but only if they’re constrained by strict risk control and harsh assumptions. When ideas like this are stress-tested properly in tools like WealthLab with pessimistic fills and regime variation, most “smart” models flatten fast. Keep sizing small and let it run across different volatility environments. If it remains stable beyond a short window, then you’re building a system, not just surviving a friendly regime.

comments.author: Shekher_05
comments.body: yeah that transition from backtest to live is brutal. spreads and slippage expose weaknesses real fast. ensemble setups seem to handle that a bit better from what i've seen. letting different models deal with different parts of the market instead of forcing one system to do everything. even came across something called profitradingterminal doing multi-model signal aggregation like that. feels like a more realistic direction than chasing the “perfect” single model tbh.
